reviews.why: Same metric type and mechanism, but DIFFERENT scope, so the three-part test fails.  Object/metric: SAME — both measure average weekly sales per restaurant (sales volume per operating week). Mechanism: SAME — average sales generated per restaurant per operating week. Scope: DIFFERENT — this is the deciding factor. - "average_weekly_sales" evidence is for The Cheesecake Factory brand: $239,679/week ("Cheesecake Factory average sales per restaurant operating week"). - "flower_child_average_weekly_sales" evidence is explicitly for Flower Child, a separate restaurant brand owned by CAKE (Cheesecake Factory parent): $85,200/week ("Flower Child average weekly sales").  The two figures differ ~3x ($239,679 vs $85,200) precisely because they cover different brand/restaurant concepts. Per the project rule that a brand gets its own driver (brand=own-driver), a brand-specific metric must stay separate from the parent/flagship-brand metric. Merging would wrongly roll up Flower Child's sales into the general/Cheesecake-Factory average. Scope mismatch → keep separate, no SAME_AS.

Token overlap on "mix"/"sales" only. They measure two distinct revenue segments of CAKE's sales pie. A = bar (on-premise alcohol/beverage at the bar counter, ~23% of mix, "Bar mix is about 23%"). B = delivery (off-premise delivery channel, ~10% of mix, "10% is delivery... rest split through online ordering and phone pickup"). Three-part test fails: (1) same object — NO, bar revenue vs delivery revenue are different metrics; (2) same scope — different segments (~23% vs ~10%); (3) same mechanism — NO, on-premise bar purchases vs off-premise delivery, opposite channels. Keep separate.

Fails the same-scope test. "consumer_spending" is the broad, general consumer — overall confidence and spending across the whole customer base (10-Q/8-K risk language: "conditions that impact consumer confidence and spending"; transcript talk of general economic pressure and trade-down). "high_income_consumer_spending" is a narrow sub-segment: the top-end / $100K+ income cohort specifically. The b-side evidence is explicitly about that affluent tier ("top end of consumers really does feel pressure... they move down off the steakhouses"; "income of 100 plus K, those jobs are going away"). Object/metric (spending) is shared, but scope differs (all consumers vs high-income subset), and the mechanism differs too — the high-income driver hinges on a trade-DOWN benefit (affluent diners shift from pricier steakhouses to Cheesecake Factory), which is a distinct, even partly opposite, dynamic from general consumer-spending softness hurting traffic. Token overlap is expected (both contain "consumer/spending") but the high-income variant is a deliberately separate, more specific driver. Keep separate.

These are two unrelated concepts that only share the surface tokens "tip" and "credit." Different OBJECT: A = the FICA tip tax credit (a federal income-tax credit employers claim for payroll taxes paid on employee tip income; evidence is all about the SITCA program and losing "tax credits"). B = the tip credit wage (a labor/minimum-wage rule letting employers pay tipped workers a lower base wage, offset by tips). They are not the same metric. Different MECHANISM: A is a tax offset that reduces taxes owed; B is a wage-rate floor that affects labor cost. A's risk is losing a tax credit; B's risk is minimum-wage/tip-credit-wage rate increases or elimination raising wages paid. Different financial impact path: A flows through income-tax expense, B flows through labor/operating expense. The "credit" in A means a tax credit; the "credit" in B is part of the term "tip credit wage" (a wage concept), so the shared word has opposite meanings. Token overlap is a coincidence of vocabulary, not shared meaning. Keep separate.

These two drivers share only the brand name "Flower Child" — the token overlap that triggered the suggestion — but describe entirely different business objects, scopes, and mechanisms.  OBJECT (FAIL): "flower_child_acquisition" is about CAKE (Cheesecake Factory) bringing the Flower Child brand fully under its corporate umbrella — a corporate ownership/M&A event ("bringing it fully under our umbrella"). "flower_child_off_premise_mix" is about the channel sales-mix metric at Flower Child — specifically that 55% of sales are off-premise (takeout/delivery vs dine-in). One is an acquisition event; the other is an operating sales-channel ratio. Different objects.  SCOPE (FAIL): Acquisition scope = the whole brand/entity ownership level. Off-premise-mix scope = day-to-day store-level channel performance. Different scope.  MECHANISM (FAIL): Acquisition affects financials via consolidation/ownership change (and timing of integration). Off-premise mix affects financials via revenue channel composition, margins, and throughput. Completely different causal mechanisms.  All three required conditions (same object, same scope, same mechanism) are false. The only thing in common is the "Flower Child" brand name, which is not sufficient for SAME. Keep them separate; do NOT add SAME_AS.

Same brand (Flower Child, owned by CAKE), but different object/metric and mechanism. (a) flower_child_acquisition is about bringing the Flower Child business fully under CAKE's ownership/umbrella — a corporate ownership event ("on track with Flower Child, bringing it fully under our umbrella"). (b) flower_child_sales is the recurring revenue metric of that brand (e.g., "Flower Child sales increased 11.0% to $127.5 million"). Object differs: ownership/consolidation event vs sales revenue. Mechanism differs: completing an acquisition stake vs ongoing top-line growth from operating the restaurants. Fails check 1 (same object/metric) and check 3 (same mechanism). The token overlap (flower, child) only reflects the shared brand name, not a shared driver. Keep separate.

Shared tokens flag the same brand (Flower Child), not the same driver. A is a one-time corporate transaction: "bringing it fully under our umbrella" = CAKE acquiring/consolidating ownership of the brand (M&A / capital-allocation mechanism). B is recurring operating performance: every quote is "Flower Child comparable sales increased X%" = same-store sales demand growth (consumer-demand mechanism). Fails all three tests: object differs (ownership deal vs comp-sales %), scope differs (one-off corporate event vs recurring quarterly metric), mechanism differs (deal/ownership vs restaurant demand). They can move independently (good acquisition can coincide with weak comps, or vice versa). Brand=own-driver rule says a shared brand name alone does not make two drivers the same. Keep separate.

Same brand (Flower Child), but different object/metric and mechanism. (A) flower_child_acquisition = the M&A event of bringing Flower Child fully under CAKE's umbrella — a one-time ownership/consolidation action (evidence: "bringing it fully under our umbrella"). (B) flower_child_unit_count = the store-count and unit-growth metric (38 locations, ~700 potential, ~20% annual unit growth). Object differs: corporate ownership event vs. number of restaurant locations. Mechanism differs: acquiring/consolidating a business vs. opening new stores over time. Token overlap on "flower/child" only reflects the shared brand, not the same driver. Check 1 (same object/metric) fails and check 3 (same mechanism) fails, so DIFFERENT. Note b lists same_as_variant flower_child_unit_development, which is consistent with b being the unit-growth driver, not the acquisition.

Token overlap (flower/child/unit) is misleading. These measure two different objects.  Side A = average unit volume (AUV): how much revenue ONE Flower Child location makes per year. Evidence: "$4.4 million average unit volume" and "potential average volumes for a fully mature box." Object = dollars per store. Mechanism = per-restaurant sales productivity.  Side B = unit count: HOW MANY Flower Child locations exist/can exist. Evidence: "operate 38 Flower Child locations... potential for approximately 700 domestic locations... 20% average annual unit growth." Object = number of stores. Mechanism = store expansion/footprint.  Test results: 1. Same object/metric? NO — dollars-per-store (sales) vs count-of-stores (footprint). 2. Same scope? NO — A is per-store economics; B is total network size. 3. Same mechanism? NO — A driven by sales productivity; B driven by new-store openings.  All three fail. Keep separate. (Note: B's same_as_variant "flower_child_unit_development" correctly groups with unit count, not with AUV.)

Token overlap is only the shared brand name "Flower Child"; the two drivers describe different things. A ("we continue down the integration path") is operational integration of the Flower Child concept into CAKE — object = brand-integration effort, scope = whole-business rollup, mechanism = operational integration. B ("a traditional rewards program... app based program that has points") is a customer loyalty/points feature — object = rewards program, scope = one marketing/loyalty feature, mechanism = app-based points to drive repeat visits. All three tests fail: not the same object/metric, not the same scope, not the same mechanism. Keep separate.

Token overlap is only the shared brand name "Flower Child" (a CAKE restaurant). The two drivers track different objects/metrics. (A) flower_child_margin: profitability — "on track to get to the margin target... mid-teens level... growth vehicle." Object = margin/profit trajectory. (B) flower_child_rewards_program: a customer loyalty feature — "much more of a traditional rewards program, app-based program that has points." Object = a loyalty/rewards app. Fails test 1 (different object: profit margin vs. loyalty program) and test 3 (different mechanism: cost/profitability lever vs. customer-retention app). Same brand scope, but object and mechanism differ, so keep separate.

The token overlap ("flower", "child") is from the shared brand name "Flower Child" (a CAKE restaurant concept), but the two drivers describe different objects/metrics with different mechanisms. (a) flower_child_rewards_program: evidence quote "flower child is much more of a traditional rewards program it's an app based program that has points" — this is a loyalty/rewards PROGRAM, a customer-retention mechanism. (b) flower_child_sales: all 7 evidence quotes are revenue figures ("Flower Child sales increased 11.0% to $127.5 million...", "$34.5 million for the first quarter...") — this is the SALES/REVENUE metric of the Flower Child brand. Test 1 (same object/metric): FALSE — a rewards program vs a sales/revenue figure are different objects. Test 3 (same mechanism): FALSE — points-based loyalty engagement vs top-line revenue generation. They share only the brand name, not the underlying driver. Keep separate.

Token overlap on "flower" and "child" only — both are about the Flower Child restaurant brand, but they measure different things. Side A = the loyalty/rewards program ("an app based program that has points"), a marketing/retention mechanism. Side B = comparable (same-store) sales growth for Flower Child ("Flower Child comparable sales increased approximately 6%"), a revenue performance metric. They fail all three tests: different object/metric (a rewards app vs. a comp-sales figure), different scope (a single program vs. the whole concept's sales), and different mechanism (customer loyalty driver vs. demand/traffic sales growth). Same brand, different driver. Keep separate.

Token overlap (program, rewards) only. The two drivers fail all three SAME tests:  1. SAME OBJECT/METRIC — FALSE. Side A is the existence/description of Flower Child's loyalty offering ("flower child is much more of a traditional rewards program... app based program that has points"). Side B is a financial magnitude — the margin/earnings CONTRIBUTION of a loyalty program ("about 100 basis points from a contribution perspective... from loyalty?"). One is a program; the other is a quantified P&L impact metric (basis points). Different objects.  2. SAME SCOPE — FALSE/UNCLEAR. Side A is scoped specifically to the Flower Child brand's rewards program. Side B refers to "loyalty" contribution at a broader level with no brand named — and CAKE (Cheesecake Factory) operates multiple brands. The ~100 bps loyalty contribution is not tied to Flower Child specifically and most plausibly refers to the larger flagship loyalty program, not Flower Child's app.  3. SAME MECHANISM — FALSE. Side A = a points-based app loyalty program (what it is). Side B = the financial contribution mechanism (how much loyalty adds to results in basis points). Describing a program is a different mechanism than measuring its margin contribution.  Also note the dates differ (2025-07-29 vs 2025-10-28). Merging these would conflate a brand-specific program description with a company-level loyalty-contribution metric — exactly the kind of over-merge to avoid. Keep separate.

These are two distinct metrics for the same brand (Flower Child), failing the "same object/metric" and "same scope" tests.  OBJECT/METRIC differs: - Side A = total/absolute Flower Child sales (a dollar revenue figure). Quotes: "Flower Child sales increased 11.0% to $127.5 million", "totaled $32.2 million", "sales per operating week were $80,000". This is the full top-line sales of the concept (includes ALL units, new + old). - Side B = Flower Child COMPARABLE sales (same-store sales). Quotes: "Flower Child comparable sales increased approximately 6%", "comps continue to be the strongest", "comparable sales increasing by 5%". This is a same-store / like-for-like growth metric.  SCOPE differs: - Total sales counts every restaurant including newly opened locations. - Comparable (same-store) sales deliberately EXCLUDES new units to isolate organic growth of the existing base. In restaurant reporting these are standard, separate line items that move independently.  MECHANISM differs: - Total sales growth can be driven by unit expansion (opening new Flower Child locations). - Same-store/comparable sales strips out new-unit effects to measure traffic/check/menu-price growth at existing stores only.  Evidence: Side A reports dollar amounts and operating-week figures (absolute); Side B reports only percentage comps vs prior-year same stores. A growing chain can show strong total sales while flat comps, or vice versa, so collapsing them would lose a real, separately-tracked distinction. Note: one Side-B transcript quote says "Fire Child comps" (apparent transcription typo for Flower Child) — does not change the analysis. Keep separate; do not add SAME_AS.

Both count restaurant units for CAKE (Cheesecake Factory), but they track DIFFERENT objects/scopes. Side A is the unit count for the "Flower Child" concept (38 locations, ~700 potential, ~20% annual growth). Side B is the unit count for the aggregate "Other FRC" (Fox Restaurant Concepts) portfolio (10-15% annual growth). Flower Child is one specific brand; FRC is a separate, broader portfolio of other concepts. Fails test #1 (different object — Flower Child brand vs FRC portfolio) and test #2 (different scope — single concept vs aggregate multi-concept portfolio), shown by different growth targets (20% vs 10-15%). Token overlap on "count"/"unit" only reflects that both are unit-count metrics, not the same metric. Keep separate.
Different brands counted: Flower Child vs North Italia.

The token overlap (card, gift) is a false signal. The two drivers describe unrelated objects, scopes, and mechanisms.  Driver A (gift_card_inventory_writedown): "a non-recurring $7.9 million write-down of gift card inventory." Object = the physical gift card stock/inventory (the plastic cards themselves). Mechanism = a one-time accounting write-down reducing the carrying value of that inventory asset. Scope = a single non-recurring charge.  Driver B (gift_card_redemptions): "higher gift card redemptions and lower accrued incentive compensation." Object = redemption activity (customers spending loaded gift card balances). Mechanism = recognition of revenue/breakage as customers redeem outstanding gift card liability. Scope = ongoing operating activity, not a one-off charge.  Three-part test: 1. Same object/metric? NO. A = physical card inventory asset; B = customer redemption transactions/liability. 2. Same scope? NO. A = single non-recurring write-down; B = recurring redemption flow. 3. Same mechanism? NO. A = inventory impairment (expense); B = revenue/breakage recognition (income-side).  All three fail. These are distinct drivers that happen to share the words "gift" and "card." Keep separate.  Note: A's same_as_variants ["gift_card_inventory_write_down"] is a legitimate spelling-variant merge within A, unrelated to B.

Same metric family (same-store sales / comps), but scope and mechanism differ. SCOPE: A is explicitly international — "comp positive in all those different geographies... everywhere around the world." B is the company's overall comp guidance (1%-2% range, implied 2026 number), which for CAKE centers on the core/domestic business, not the small international franchise segment. MECHANISM: A describes realized regional performance (comps already achieved). B is forward-looking guidance/assumptions and expected ranges ("our comp assumptions," "expect to be in that 1% to 2% range," "implied 2026 number," "finished near the high end of our guide"). Realized international results vs. company-wide forward guidance are two distinct drivers; the token overlap (sales/same/store) is purely lexical. Tests 2 (scope) and 3 (mechanism) fail, so keep separate.

Token overlap on "marketing" only — the meanings diverge on all three tests. OBJECT: "marketing_costs" is a spend/expense line item (operating-expense dollars tied to the Cheesecake Rewards loyalty program), while "social_media_marketing" comes from a competitive-risk statement about rivals' use of social media and the difficulty of differentiating the company's social-media messaging — that is a channel/risk factor, not a cost metric. SCOPE: marketing_costs is firm-wide operating expense affecting margins; social_media_marketing is about one promotional channel and competitive positioning. MECHANISM: marketing_costs moves results by raising operating expenses (margin pressure); social_media_marketing's mechanism is brand differentiation/competition, with no cost figure attached. None of object, scope, or mechanism match, so keep them separate.

Fails the "same scope" test. "menu_mix" is the company-wide (consolidated) menu-mix driver for CAKE — its evidence is all total-company average-check / sales-mix language (e.g. "a 1.9% negative change from menu mix", "Average check variations are driven by menu price increases and/or changes in menu mix"). "north_italia_menu_mix" is scoped to ONE brand/segment, North Italia, with its own segment-level number ("The north Italia mix was negative 2%"). Same object (menu mix %) and same mechanism (customers buying higher/lower-priced items), but the scope differs: whole company vs. a single brand. North Italia is a sub-component, not a synonym, of the consolidated driver, so merging would erase a real segment-vs-total distinction. Keep separate (DIFFERENT). A directional/parent-child link could be considered later, but they are not the same driver.

Same object/metric (net income margin) and same scope (CAKE company-level, fiscal periods), but the MECHANISM differs. (a) net_income_margin_guidance = forward-looking management projection: "we expect/anticipate fiscal 2023 net income margin of approximately 4%" — what the company forecasts before the period. (b) net_income_margin_surprise = a backward-looking beat/miss outcome measuring actual result vs prior guidance: "Adjusted net income margin of 5% was above our expectations," "exceeded the high end of the guidance ranges." Guidance is the forecast input; surprise is the realized-vs-expected delta. These are distinct economic events with opposite time orientation (forecast issuance vs. earnings beat/miss), so they fail the same-mechanism test. Merging them would collapse a forward projection with a post-fact beat/miss signal — different price-driver behavior. Keep separate.

Different object/metric and different mechanism. (1) Object/metric: side A measures capital expenditures (dollars spent building new restaurants — $61.6M, $76.1M), a cash-outflow/investment figure. Side B is about restaurant-level margin (profitability) and how fast new restaurants ramp to mature margins — a profitability metric. CapEx (money spent) and margin (profit %) are entirely different financial objects. (2) Mechanism: A is spending to develop/open locations; B is operational ramp-up improving early-stage restaurant margins over time. Only the shared tokens "new" and "restaurant" link them, which is surface overlap, not the same underlying driver. They must stay separate.

Different metrics despite shared brand+word "average". A = average check: dollars one customer spends per visit (~$34.60 lunch / $44.40 dinner). B = AUV (average unit volume): total annual sales per restaurant location (~$8.0M / $7.7M). Fails all three tests — (1) object: per-customer spend vs per-store annual revenue; (2) scope: one transaction/guest vs one restaurant per year; (3) mechanism: menu price x items per guest vs traffic x check x operating days across the whole store. Magnitudes differ by ~10^5 ($34 vs $8M). Token overlap (average/italia/north) is just the brand name plus a coincidental common word. Keep separate.

These are two distinct metrics for the North Italia brand that only share brand-name tokens ("north", "italia"). Object/metric: FAILS — "average_check" is the dollar amount spent per customer per visit ($34.60 lunch / $44.40 dinner per the 10-K), while "menu_mix" is a same-store-sales component measuring the sales contribution from menu shifts (the transcript: "north Italia mix was negative 2%"). Average check is a price/spend-per-guest figure; menu mix is a sales-growth decomposition factor — fundamentally different objects. Mechanism: FAILS — check size is driven by what each guest orders and prices paid; menu mix reflects period-over-period change in product/category sales blend. Same brand and company (CAKE, North Italia) but not the same metric or mechanism. Keep separate.

These are two different metrics that only share the brand token "north italia". Object/metric: A measures AVERAGE CHECK (dollar amount per customer per visit — $34.60 lunch, $44.40 dinner), while B measures RESTAURANT-LEVEL MARGIN (a profitability percentage — 13.3%, 15%). One is a revenue-per-ticket figure, the other is a profit ratio; they are not the same object. Mechanism: average check is driven by menu pricing, mix, and add-on items per guest; restaurant-level margin is driven by the relationship between sales and store-level costs (labor, food, occupancy). Even though a menu price increase can lift both, they fail criterion 1 (same object) and criterion 3 (same mechanism). Token overlap on the brand name is the only link. Keep separate.

Different objects/metrics. A = average unit volume (AUV): average sales dollars per restaurant location (~$8.0M, $7.7M annualized). B = restaurant count: the number of North Italia company-owned restaurants (a unit count). The token overlap ("north", "italia") only shares the brand name, not the measured quantity. A measures sales-per-unit (dollars/mechanism = revenue density), B measures how many units exist (a count). Fails test 1 (same object) and test 3 (same mechanism): dollars-per-location vs. number-of-locations are distinct metrics. Keep separate.

Different object/metric and different mechanism. A = Average Unit Volume (AUV): a sales/revenue figure measured in dollars per location ("$8.0 million", "annualized AUVs of $7.7 million"). B = restaurant-level (four-wall) margin: a profitability percentage ("13.3%", "15%", "250 basis point improvement"). Same brand (North Italia) and overlapping scope (adjusted mature locations), but revenue-per-store and profit-margin-percent are distinct objects with distinct mechanisms (top-line volume vs. cost/profitability). The token overlap is only the brand name "North Italia." Fails check 1 (object) and check 3 (mechanism), so keep separate.

Different metric and mechanism; only the brand token "north_italia" overlaps. A = Average Unit Volume: absolute dollar sales per restaurant location (evidence "$8.0 million for fiscal 2022", "annualized AUVs of $7.7 million") = a per-unit dollar-level computed as total sales / number of units. B = same-store/comparable sales: year-over-year percentage change on a fixed restaurant base (evidence "comparable sales increased approximately 8%", "vs. prior year 9 %", "decreased approximately 2%") = a growth-rate delta. Fails test 1 (different object: dollar level-per-unit vs % growth) and test 3 (different mechanism: sales/units productivity vs period-over-period delta). These are two standard, independent restaurant KPIs that move independently. Token overlap (italia, north) is not meaning equivalence. Keep separate.

These share the brand "North Italia" but measure two completely different objects. (A) menu_mix evidence: "The north Italia mix was negative 2%" — this is a sales/menu mix metric (a percentage shift in what customers order or same-store sales mix). (B) restaurant_count evidence: "North Italia Company-Owned Restaurants" — this is a unit count of physical locations. Check 1 (same object/metric): FALSE — a mix percentage vs a count of restaurants are different metrics. Check 3 (same mechanism): FALSE — menu/sales mix changes with customer ordering and pricing; restaurant count changes with openings/closings of units. Token overlap on the brand name ("italia", "north") drove the suggestion, but the underlying metrics are distinct. Keep separate.

These two drivers share the brand "North Italia" and overlapping tokens (north, italia), which is why the suggester flagged them. But they measure different objects with different mechanisms.  OBJECT/METRIC (fails test 1): - Side A (north_italia_menu_mix): measures "mix" — the sales-mix shift, stated as "negative 2%." This is a sales/traffic composition figure (how the menu blend of items sold is shifting), expressed as a percent change. - Side B (north_italia_restaurant_level_margin): measures restaurant-level / four-wall profit margin, stated as "13.3%" and "15%." This is a profitability ratio (margin as a percent of sales), explicitly about "restaurant-level margin" / "Forewall margin."  A sales-mix percent and a profit-margin percent are different metrics entirely.  MECHANISM (fails test 3): - Menu mix reflects what customers order and how that blend changes period over period. - Restaurant-level margin reflects cost structure and profitability, "supported by a menu price increase." Note: menu PRICE increase (a pricing lever affecting margin) is not the same as menu MIX (composition of items sold). Margin can be driven by mix, price, labor, and food cost — mix is at most one input, not the metric itself.  SCOPE: Both are scoped to North Italia, so test 2 (scope) is arguably satisfied — but tests 1 and 3 both fail, so the verdict is DIFFERENT.  The same_as_variants on side B (north_italia_four_wall_margin, north_italia_margin, north_italia_restaurant_margin) confirm side B's cluster is purely about margin, not mix. Merging menu mix into the margin driver would conflate a composition metric with a profitability metric — exactly the kind of over-merge to avoid. Keep them separate.

Token overlap (north, italia) only proves both concern the same North Italia brand — same scope. But they measure different objects/mechanisms. A = menu/sales mix (revenue composition: "the north Italia mix was negative 2%"). B = segment margins (segment profitability: "North Italia segment margins... mature units within that"). Test 1 fails: sales-mix % vs operating-margin % are distinct metrics. Test 3 fails: menu-mix shifts revenue composition while margin reflects cost/profit structure; mix can move opposite to margin. Only scope matches, so DIFFERENT — keep separate.

Token overlap on "north_italia" only — same brand, different driver. Side A is about menu PRICING: quotes discuss price increases ("another price increase in October") and being "behind on pricing." Side B is a restaurant UNIT COUNT KPI ("North Italia Company-Owned Restaurants", fiscal.ai-kpi). Fails all three tests: (1) different object/metric — price level vs. store count; (2) different scope — menu pricing action vs. physical footprint; (3) different mechanism — pricing power/inflation vs. unit growth/expansion. Same company (CAKE) and same brand do not make them the same driver. Keep separate.

These are two distinct concepts that share the "North Italia" brand only. (a) north_italia_menu_pricing = the act/decision of raising menu prices (a pricing-action driver: "another price increase in October," "were you behind on pricing there?"). (b) north_italia_restaurant_level_margin = a profitability metric, the four-wall/restaurant-level margin percentage ("margin... improved to 13.3%," "restaurant-level margin... was 15%, a 250 bp improvement"). Test 1 (same object/metric) FAILS: one is a price-increase action, the other is a margin ratio. Test 3 (same mechanism) FAILS: pricing is an input lever; margin is an outcome influenced by many factors (pricing, costs, traffic, mix). The margin quote even names the price increase as merely one of several supports ("supported by a menu price increase"), confirming pricing is a contributor to margin, not the same thing. Token overlap on "north,italia" is just the shared brand. Keep separate.

Token overlap (italia, north) is just the shared brand name, not a sign of the same driver. The two measure different things:  A = north_italia_menu_pricing: tracks PRICE INCREASES the company takes (the lever it pulls). Evidence: "we have another price increase in October... for North Italia" and "the pricing you just took... were you behind on pricing there?"  B = north_italia_same_store_sales: tracks COMPARABLE RESTAURANT SALES (the outcome/result, % change vs prior year). 20+ evidence refs all of the form "North Italia comparable sales increased/decreased approximately X%".  Three-part test: 1. Same object/metric? NO. A = menu price change (an action/input). B = comparable same-store sales growth (a reported result metric). 2. Same scope? Same brand (North Italia) and company (CAKE), but different financial concept. 3. Same mechanism? NO. Pricing is one input that partly drives comp sales, but comp sales also reflect traffic, mix, new units lapping, etc. Pricing ≠ comp sales; one is a cause-side lever, the other is an outcome KPI.  Since criteria 1 and 3 are false, verdict is DIFFERENT. Keep separate. Note: B already correctly lists "north_italia_comparable_sales" as a same_as variant, which confirms B is the comp-sales driver and A is not part of it.

Token overlap (italia/margin/north) suggested the pair, but the evidence shows two different metrics at different levels. A = "restaurant-level margin" / "four-wall margin" for "adjusted mature North Italia locations" — store-level profitability (sales minus store operating costs, excluding corporate/segment overhead), and scoped to the MATURE subset only (13.3% in 2022, 15% in 2024). B = "North Italia SEGMENT margins" — a segment-level reporting measure covering the whole North Italia segment, not just mature stores. Test 1 (same object/metric) fails: restaurant-level/four-wall margin excludes segment overhead, whereas segment margin is a distinct higher-level reporting line. Test 2 (same scope) fails: A is the adjusted-mature subset, B is the full segment — B's own quote asks about "mature units WITHIN that [segment]," explicitly treating mature units as a subset of the segment. Since at least one (here two) of the three conditions is false, verdict is DIFFERENT; keep them separate.

Token overlap is only the brand name "North Italia"; the actual metrics are unrelated.  Side A = restaurant-level / four-wall MARGIN (profitability %): "restaurant-level margin... was 15%, a 250 bps improvement", "Four-wall margin... improved to 13.3%". This measures cost/profit efficiency.  Side B = TRAFFIC (guest count / foot traffic, a volume metric): "Traffic was negative 4%", "traffic was negative 6%", broken out alongside price and mix as sales-comp components.  Test the three gates: 1. Same object/metric? NO — margin (a percentage of sales kept as profit) vs traffic (number of guests). Different units, different things. 2. Same scope? Both are North Italia brand, but the metric subject differs (profitability vs guest count). 3. Same mechanism? NO — margin is driven by menu price, labor, and food cost; traffic is driven by demand/guest visits. Margin can rise while traffic falls (and the evidence shows exactly that: 2024 margin +250bps while 2025 traffic -4%).  Gate 1 and gate 3 both fail. These are two distinct drivers for the same brand. Keep separate.

Same metric (restaurant-level / four-wall margin) but DIFFERENT scope. Driver A is scoped to the North Italia concept specifically (evidence: "margin for the adjusted mature North Italia locations" at 13.3% and 15%; its same_as_variants are all north_italia_* names). Driver B is scoped to The Cheesecake Factory concept and the overall portfolio (evidence: "increase restaurant-level margins at The Cheesecake Factory concept," "Cheesecake Factory restaurant level margins ... 18.4%," general "longer-term restaurant-level margin objectives"). CAKE runs multiple distinct concepts and reports their margins separately — North Italia (~13-15%) is a different business unit with a different margin level than Cheesecake Factory (~16-18%). Token overlap (restaurant, level, margin) is purely lexical. Scope test fails, so keep separate to preserve the concept-level distinction and avoid an over-merge that erases real signal.

Token overlap (north, italia) only flags the same brand/segment, not the same metric. The three-part test fails on all counts:  1. SAME OBJECT/METRIC — FALSE. "a" measures segment MARGIN (profitability of the North Italia segment). "b" measures TRAFFIC (guest/customer count change). Margin is a profit metric; traffic is a volume metric. Different objects.  2. SAME SCOPE — Both are North Italia brand, so scope (the brand) overlaps. But margin is segment-level profitability while traffic is a sales-driver component. Same brand, different financial line.  3. SAME MECHANISM — FALSE. Margin moves via cost structure, pricing, and mature-unit mix (evidence "a": "North Italia segment margins... mature units"). Traffic moves via guest count / footfall (evidence "b": "Traffic was negative 4%", "traffic was negative 6%"). Completely different mechanisms.  Evidence quotes confirm the split: "a" asks about segment margins and mature units (profitability), while "b" repeatedly reports traffic vs. price vs. mix as distinct comp-sales components. Traffic is even broken out alongside price and mix in the same sentence, showing it is a specific driver, not a margin proxy. Keep separate.
DIFFERENT — promotions is a marketing input/lever; sales is the resulting channel revenue metric. Different object and mechanism. Token overlap only.
